title: Writing - Part 1: Mental Health Awareness
description: Your class has attended a talk about mental health and the ways in which society should do more to support people who struggle.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the approaches in your notes. You should explain which approach you think would be most beneficial, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Young people should be able to talk about their feelings without fear of being judged.
Employers have a responsibility to protect their employees' mental wellbeing.
More public investment in mental health services would reduce long waiting times.

structure.intro: Introduce the importance of mental health and the two approaches you will discuss.
structure.body1: Discuss the first approach, giving reasons why it could be effective.
structure.body2: Discuss the second approach and compare its benefits to the first.
structure.conclusion: Summarise both approaches and state which you consider most beneficial.

content.modelAnswer:
Mental health is one of the most important aspects of overall wellbeing, yet it has historically been neglected. Two approaches that could significantly improve the situation are better support in schools and increased government funding for mental health services.

Introducing mental health education and counselling in schools would help young people manage stress and emotional difficulties from an early age. If pupils learn to recognise warning signs and feel safe discussing their struggles, they are more likely to seek help before problems become serious. A supportive school environment could truly change lives.

Government funding, however, targets a broader section of society. With more investment, mental health services could reduce waiting times and reach people who might not otherwise receive support. Greater availability of therapists and community programmes would benefit people of all ages.

In my opinion, while both approaches are valuable, increasing government funding is the more impactful solution. Schools can provide early support, but ultimately it is well-resourced national services that ensure everyone, regardless of age or background, has access to the help they need.
